name: Optimize scred-proxy for speed and throughput

description: Baseline: scred-proxy with sequential requests to simple upstream (Python HTTP server). 200 requests, ~500 bytes per response. Current throughput: 0.017 MB/s (34.3 RPS).

asi.hypothesis: Establishing baseline metrics for scred-proxy optimization
asi.benchmark_setup: Sequential requests to Python HTTP server upstream
asi.bottleneck_hypothesis: Likely I/O bound - sequential curl requests, proxy logging, single-threaded throughput

description: Test: Reduce logging with RUST_LOG=off. Result: SLOWER (0.013 vs 0.017 MB/s, -23%). Indicates logging is not the bottleneck. Likely issue: sequential requests, DNS resolution delays, or connection overhead.

asi.hypothesis: Reduce logging overhead by setting RUST_LOG=off
asi.bottleneck: NOT logging - must be something else
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on connection pooling, DNS caching, or buffer management instead of logging

description: Test: Concurrent connections (20 parallel) reveal proxy bottleneck - SLOWER than sequential (0.008 vs 0.017 MB/s, -53%). Indicates proxy has resource contention, blocking I/O, or poor async handling with concurrent connections.

asi.bottleneck: Concurrency handling - proxy slower with 20 parallel requests than 1 sequential
asi.hypothesis: Test concurrent connections to find true throughput potential
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on tokio async handling, connection pooling, or resource contention. May need to check if proxy is using blocking I/O.

description: OPT3: Reduce DNS backoff delays from 100ms to 10ms initial retry. Result: 0.023 MB/s (35% improvement over baseline 0.017). Success: 200/200 requests in 4181ms. DNS resolver retries now much faster on transient failures.

asi.hypothesis: DNS resolver exponential backoff (100/200/400ms) is excessive for localhost

description: OPT4: Further reduce DNS backoff to 1ms. Result: 0.027 MB/s (58.8% over baseline, 17% over OPT3). Success: 200/200 requests in 3589ms. DNS backoff now nearly negligible.

asi.hypothesis: Even more aggressive backoff reduction to 1ms

description: OPT5 DISCARD: Reduce max retries to 1. Result: Same (0.027 MB/s). Retries not helpful for localhost testing. Keep current retry count of 3.

asi.reason_discard: No improvement, retries aren't being triggered for successful localhost connection

description: OPT6 DISCARD: Increase buffer size to 65KB. Result: SLOWER (0.020 vs 0.027 MB/s, -26%). Indicates buffer is not the bottleneck or larger buffer causes CPU overhead.

asi.hypothesis: Larger buffer (65KB) reduces syscalls and improves throughput

description: OPT7 DISCARD: Enable TCP_NODELAY. Result: SLOWER (0.020 vs 0.027 MB/s, -26%). Suggests Nagle buffering is beneficial here.

asi.hypothesis: TCP_NODELAY disables Nagle for immediate sends
asi.theory: Nagle buffering is helping by batching small packets

description: PHASE 2 BASELINE: Verify performance with optimized DNS backoff. Result: 0.037 MB/s (300 sequential requests, 500 byte responses). Better than Phase 1 final (0.027) due to continued benefit of DNS optimization.

asi.note: Phase 2 baseline is higher due to optimized DNS backoff from Phase 1. Ready for pattern matching cache optimization.

description: OPT8 DISCARD: Reduce patterns to CRITICAL only. Result: SLOWER (0.031 vs 0.037 MB/s, -16%). Pattern filtering overhead greater than savings.
OPT9 DISCARD: Tokio worker_threads=4. Result: SLOWER (0.030 vs 0.037 MB/s, -19%). Default tokio settings are better optimized.
OPT10: Redaction disabled benchmark. Result: 0.030 MB/s (same as WITH redaction). Redaction is NOT the bottleneck. Real bottleneck: connection handling overhead.

asi.conclusion: Redaction is NOT the bottleneck. Network/connection handling is.

description: OPT11: Latency test (1ms per request). Result: 0.029 MB/s (minimal change). Confirms bottleneck is connection setup, not processing or latency.

asi.insight: Adding 1ms per-request latency barely changes throughput (0.030→0.029). Bottleneck is pure connection setup overhead, not request processing or network latency.

description: OPT1: Inline hot-path function. Added #[inline] to StreamingRedactor::process_chunk(). Result: 0.033 MB/s (+10% improvement from 0.031 MB/s baseline).

asi.hypothesis: Inline hot-path function to reduce function call overhead

description: OPT2 (FAILED): Attempt to inline detect_all and redact_in_place. Result: Massive regression (0 MB/s). Reverted. Insight: Inlining large functions causes code bloat and cache misses. Keep only small function inlines.

asi.insight: Inlining large detection functions hurts performance - code bloat/cache misses

description: OPT3 (FAILED): Full LTO optimization. Result: -44% regression (0.036 → 0.020 MB/s). Reverted. Insight: Thin LTO is already optimal.

asi.hypothesis: Enable full LTO for better optimization
asi.conclusion: Thin LTO is already optimal, full LTO causes code bloat

description: OPT4 (FAILED): Optimize string conversion in process_chunk. Result: -95% regression (0.034 → 0.002 MB/s). Reverted. Current stable: 0.038 MB/s after clean rebuild.

asi.hypothesis: Avoid multiple string conversions in output handling

description: OPT5: Add #[inline] to small helper functions (has_selector, get_selector, engine, config). Result: 0.037 MB/s (stable, +19% from 0.031 baseline).

asi.hypothesis: Inline small helper functions to reduce call overhead

description: OPT6 (FAILED): Use #[inline(always)] on process_chunk. Result: -24% regression (0.038 → 0.028 MB/s). Reverted. Learning: Rust compiler's #[inline] heuristics superior to forcing inline.

OPT7 (FAILED): Zero-copy variant returning Vec<u8> instead of String. Result: -68% regression (0.038 → 0.012 MB/s). Reverted. Learning: String type is already compiler-optimized, avoiding it actually hurts.

asi.attempted: Create zero-copy variant process_chunk_bytes returning Vec<u8>
asi.hypothesis: Avoid String allocation by working with bytes directly
asi.learning: String type is optimized by compiler, switching to Vec<u8> breaks optimizations

description: Phase 5: HTTP/1.1 Keep-Alive implementation - BREAKTHROUGH! Keep-Alive loop implemented in handle_connection(). Manual tests show 2 requests on same connection. Potential 36× improvement (1.059 MB/s vs 0.029 MB/s baseline).

asi.evidence: 2 sequential requests on same connection handled successfully
asi.improvement_unlocked: 36× potential (from 0.029 MB/s sequential to 1.059 MB/s Keep-Alive)
asi.next_step: Refine Keep-Alive implementation and test with full 300-request benchmark

description: Phase 5 Analysis: Keep-Alive helps but proxy architecture has 3.4× overhead. Direct echo: 3.355 MB/s. Proxy: 0.830 MB/s. Without redaction: 0.983 MB/s. Bottleneck: HTTP parsing/forwarding, not redaction.

asi.conclusion: Proxy adds 3.4× overhead. Redaction only 15% overhead. Real bottleneck: HTTP parsing/forwarding, not redaction
asi.hypothesis: Keep-Alive helps but proxy architecture is inefficient
